book.title: Difficult Conversations: How to Discuss What Matters Most (3rd ed.)
book.author: Douglas Stone, Bruce Patton, Sheila Heen
book.domain_fit_note: The copilot's emotional-intelligence and de-escalation layer, not its pricing brain. Directly usable when buyers are rude, suspicious, accusatory, or something has gone wrong (item not as described, late shipping, refund/return, scam accusations). Core moves — separate intent from impact, acknowledge feelings before problem-solving, shift blame to contribution, the And Stance, reframe accusation into joint problem — map cleanly onto P2P complaint/dispute chat. Weaker for active price-winning stages.

core_thesis: Every difficult conversation is really three: the 'What Happened?' conversation (truth, intentions, blame), the Feelings conversation, and the Identity conversation (what this says about me). People make the same errors in each — assuming they're right, inventing the other's intentions, assigning blame, mishandling feelings, and letting an all-or-nothing self-image get destabilized. The fix is to drop the message-delivery stance (proving you're right / changing them) for a learning stance: get curious about their story, separate impact from intent, map joint contribution instead of blame, acknowledge feelings before problem-solving, and open from a neutral 'Third Story.' You can't control the other person's reaction — only your own clarity, curiosity, and grounding.

plays.what_it_means: When a buyer is upset, name and validate the emotion first; do not jump to the fix, refund math, or your defense. Order matters — people can't hear a solution until they feel heard.
plays.why_it_matters: Attached to every expression of feeling are invisible questions ('Do you understand? Do you care? Can I trust you?'). Until those get a 'yes,' the buyer won't take in your solution. Acknowledgment cannot be skipped; fixing too early reads as 'I don't understand how you feel.'
plays.when_to_use: Any complaint — not as described, late shipping, damaged, 'I'm so disappointed.' First reply to any angry or hurt message.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the buyer is calm and transactional ('is this available?') — acknowledging non-existent feelings sounds patronizing. Don't manufacture emotion.
plays.marketplace_mapping: First line of a Depop/Vinted/Posh DM reply to a complaint — acknowledge in sentence one, BEFORE proposing a partial refund, return label, or replacement.
plays.example_buyer_message: This jacket is NOT the color in the photos. I'm so annoyed, I waited two weeks for this.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: That's really frustrating, especially after waiting two weeks — I'd be disappointed too. I want to make this right. Can you send a quick photo in daylight so I can see what you're seeing, and we'll sort it out?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "Each side must have their feelings acknowledged before you can even start down that road. Acknowledgment is a step that simply cannot be skipped." (Ch. 5, The Importance of Acknowledgment)

plays.principle: Disentangle Intent from Impact (you're being accused of scamming)
plays.what_it_means: When a buyer accuses you of bad intent ('you scammed me', 'you knew it was fake'), separate the two hidden claims: (1) 'you meant to harm me' and (2) 'I was harmed / I'm upset.' Don't take the bait on #1; respond to #2 first. Never argue your good intentions before acknowledging their experience.
plays.why_it_matters: We attribute intent from the impact on us ('I feel cheated, therefore you cheated me'). Intentions are invisible and the guess is usually wrong. Protesting 'I'd never scam you!' before acknowledging the harm changes the topic from their hurt to your character and leaves them feeling dismissed — making them angrier.
plays.when_to_use: trust-authenticity and rude-aggressive objections; 'this is a scam,' 'you sent me a fake,' 'you did this on purpose.'
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When evidence of actual fraud is on the table and the buyer needs facts (tracking, receipts) — still acknowledge first, but don't over-apologize in a way that implies guilt you don't have.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Defusing 'scammer' accusations in chat before a dispute/case opens on eBay, Vinted, or Poshmark; buys time and reduces retaliatory negative reviews.
plays.example_buyer_message: You KNEW this bag was fake when you sold it to me. Scammer.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I hear that you think this isn't authentic and you feel cheated — that would upset anyone, and I take it seriously. I didn't sell it believing it was anything but genuine, and I want to get to the bottom of it with you. Can you send photos of the date code and the areas you're worried about? If it's not right, I'll make it right.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "accusations about our bad intentions are always made up of two separate assertions... Don't take the bait. Note the assumption, set it aside, and first work to hear the hurt or anger." (Ch. 3, Listen Past the Accusation)

plays.what_it_means: When something went wrong, drop 'whose fault is it' (judging, backward) and frame it as 'how did we each end up here and what fixes it' (understanding, forward). Map the system, not the culprit.
plays.why_it_matters: Blame invites defensiveness, denial, and an either-or fight; nobody wants the role of 'the accused.' Contribution is easier to raise and pivots naturally to problem-solving. Often both contributed (buyer didn't read measurements; you didn't list a flaw clearly).
plays.when_to_use: condition-doubt, 'doesn't fit,' 'not as described,' shipping mix-ups — shared-causation situations.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: True one-sided harm (you shipped the wrong/broken item) — own it cleanly instead. Never imply a defrauded/abused party 'contributed.'
plays.marketplace_mapping: Resolving sizing/fit and 'not as described' complaints on Depop/Vinted/Posh without anyone losing face, so the buyer accepts a partial refund or return instead of opening a case.
plays.example_buyer_message: These jeans are way too small, they don't fit at all. Not as described.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Sorry they don't fit — that's a let-down when you've been waiting. Sounds like we may have read the sizing differently: I listed the flat measurements in the description, and it's possible they ran smaller than the tag suggests. Let's sort it out — I'm happy to accept a return, or do a partial refund if you'd rather keep them. Which works better for you?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "At heart, blame is about judging and contribution is about understanding... Contribution Is About Understanding, and Looks Forward." (Ch. 4, Distinguish Blame from Contribution)

plays.principle: Open from the Third Story (when YOU raise a problem)
plays.what_it_means: When the seller needs to raise something hard (buyer slow to pay, wants to cancel, left an unfair review, keeps haggling rudely), start with a neutral description both sides can agree to — the version a mediator would give — not your accusation.
plays.why_it_matters: Opening inside your own story ('you keep lowballing me') triggers the buyer's Identity Conversation and defensiveness instantly. A Third-Story opening names the difference without judgment, so they can sign on without feeling attacked.
plays.when_to_use: stalling-ghosting follow-ups, unpaid-item nudges, contesting an unfair/mistaken review, raising repeated rude behavior.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Simple transactional asks (confirming address) — overhead. Skip if speed matters more than relationship and there's no review at stake.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Following up on an eBay unpaid Best Offer, nudging a ghosting buyer, or politely contesting a review on Posh/Depop/Vinted.
plays.example_buyer_message: (buyer committed to buy 4 days ago, hasn't paid, gone silent)
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Hi! Looks like we might have gotten our wires crossed on the timing for this one — I've got it held for you and want to make sure it still works on your end. Totally fine either way, just let me know if you'd like to go ahead or if you've changed your mind, so I can plan around it.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "every difficult conversation includes an invisible Third Story... describing the problem between the parties in a way that rings true for both sides simultaneously without taking sides." (Ch. 8, Begin from the Third Story)

plays.principle: Adopt the "And Stance" (both can be true)
plays.what_it_means: Reject the false either-or. Hold your position AND validate the buyer's feelings in one breath: 'I understand you're upset, AND I'm not able to do a full refund, AND I do want to help.'
plays.why_it_matters: The And Stance lets you assert the full strength of your view without diminishing theirs. It's the most powerful place to stand when enforcing a firm decision (declining a refund, holding a price) because it stays warm and clear at once, lowering escalation.
plays.when_to_use: Holding firm on price/policy while keeping rapport; declining a return kindly; rude-aggressive and lowball objections where you must say no without a fight.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When you actually want to concede — don't fake firmness. Don't stack so many 'ands' it sounds robotic; 2–3 max.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Declining a return on a final-sale Depop item, or holding price against an aggressive eBay Best-Offer counter, without souring the chat or risking a bad review.
plays.example_buyer_message: $15 or nothing, and honestly your prices are a joke.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I get that $15 is your budget, and I can tell you're a serious buyer — and at the same time I've priced this at $28 because of [reason]. I can do $25 if that helps. No pressure either way.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "Don't choose between the stories; embrace both. That's the And Stance... gives you a place from which to assert the full strength of your views and feelings without having to diminish the views and feelings of someone else." (Ch. 2, Adopt the "And Stance")

plays.what_it_means: Take whatever destructive thing the buyer says and translate it into a workable frame: blame -> contribution, accusation -> impact+intent, 'you're wrong' -> 'we see it differently,' then steer toward solving it together. Do it persistently, every turn if needed.
plays.why_it_matters: Reframing keeps the conversation constructive regardless of how the buyer behaves. It's the most reliable 'lead the conversation' move when the other side is stuck in blame or attack mode.
plays.when_to_use: Whenever a reply contains an accusation, an absolute ('you ALWAYS...'), or a demand for blame; ongoing dispute threads.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the buyer is already collaborative — reframing a non-hostile message is needless. Avoid stilted 'I hear that you feel...' templating.
plays.mechanic: 1) Catch the destructive frame in their message (truth-claim / accusation / blame / character attack). 2) Acknowledge the feeling underneath it (one short clause). 3) Re-state it in the target frame: different-stories, impact-not-intent, or contribution-not-blame. 4) Pivot to a joint, forward-looking question ('how do we sort this out?').
plays.marketplace_mapping: Persistently redirecting a 'this is all your fault / you're a scammer' dispute thread toward 'let's figure out a fix' before either party escalates to platform resolution.
plays.example_buyer_message: This is completely your fault and you need to refund me everything right now.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I can see you're really unhappy with how this turned out, and I want to fix it. Rather than who's at fault, let me focus on a solution: I can refund the item in full once it's back to me, or do a 30% partial if you'd rather keep it. Which would you prefer?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "Reframing means taking the essence of what the other person says and 'translating it' into concepts that are more helpful... Reframe, Reframe, Reframe." (Ch. 11, You Can Reframe Anything)

plays.principle: Move from Certainty to Curiosity ("Help me understand")
plays.what_it_means: Replace internal 'how can they be so unreasonable?' with 'I wonder what they know/experienced that I don't?' In chat, that's genuine open questions instead of defending or asserting.
plays.why_it_matters: Certainty locks you out of the buyer's story; curiosity lets you in — and it's persuasive (people open up and become more receptive once they feel you're trying to understand). It surfaces the real issue (a 'difficult' buyer is often just scared the item is fake).
plays.when_to_use: condition-doubt, vague complaints, confusing/contradictory messages, any time you're tempted to argue you're right.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When you have the facts and the buyer needs a decision, not more questions — don't 'ease in' with endless questions to dodge delivering news.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Diagnosing what a buyer actually wants before reacting — turns a heated 'this is wrong' into a specific, solvable issue on any platform.
plays.example_buyer_message: Yeah this isn't what I expected at all.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Ah, I don't want you stuck with something you're not happy with. Can you tell me more about what's off — is it the size, the condition, the color, something else? Help me understand and I'll figure out the best fix.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "Instead of asking yourself, 'How can they be so selfish?!' ask yourself, 'I wonder what information they have that I don't?'... Certainty locks us out of their story; curiosity lets us in." (Ch. 2, Move from Certainty to Curiosity)

plays.principle: Listening to Them Makes Them Listen to You
plays.what_it_means: When a buyer won't hear your explanation, the fix is usually NOT to repeat louder/more — it's to listen and paraphrase them first. People who feel unheard can't take in your message.
plays.why_it_matters: The block to their listening is that they don't feel heard. Remove it by demonstrating you understood (paraphrase + acknowledge feeling), and your explanation lands. Also makes you more persuasive ('conversational receptiveness').
plays.when_to_use: Buyer keeps repeating the same complaint; stalled dispute where you've explained your policy three times and it's not sinking in.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the buyer has understood and simply disagrees — more listening won't change a hard no; move to options/standards or alternatives.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Breaking a looping dispute on eBay/Vinted where the buyer ignores your refund-policy explanation — paraphrase their grievance once, then they engage with your terms.
plays.example_buyer_message: You're not listening, I keep saying it arrived damaged and you keep talking about your policy.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: You're right — let me make sure I've got it: it arrived damaged, that's not okay, and you want it sorted, not a policy lecture. Got it. Here's what I can actually do right now: [options]. I'd rather solve this than quote rules at you.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "the reason the other person is not listening to you is not because they are stubborn, but because they don't feel heard... the way to remove that block is by helping them feel heard." (Ch. 9, Listening to Them Helps Them Listen to You)

plays.principle: Don't Vent / Don't Hit-and-Run — Talk With Purpose
plays.what_it_means: Never fire off a reply just to feel better (venting), and never lob a half-message and leave (hit-and-run, e.g. 'Late again, eh?'). If you raise something, raise it properly with a clear constructive purpose, or don't raise it.
plays.why_it_matters: Venting and passive-aggressive jabs produce exactly the defensiveness and damage you fear, with none of the benefit. Right purposes: learn their story, express your view, problem-solve — NOT change/punish them or get it off your chest.
plays.when_to_use: Seller-coaching guardrail — when the user is angry and wants to 'let the buyer have it' or send a snippy one-liner; the copilot intercepts and reshapes.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: n/a (a safety rail that should always apply to outgoing replies).
plays.marketplace_mapping: Copilot blocks/rewrites a furious seller draft ('Wow thanks for the 1-star, real classy') into something purposeful or advises waiting, protecting the seller's reputation across reviews/feedback.
plays.example_buyer_message: (seller wants to send) Maybe actually read the description next time before crying about it
plays.recommended_agent_reply: (copilot to seller) I'd hold that one — it'll feel good for a minute but likely earns you a worse review and a dispute. Want me to draft a version that still makes your point but keeps you protected? Something like: 'I'm sorry it wasn't what you expected — the measurements were in the listing, and I'm still happy to take a return so you're not stuck with it.'
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "if your purpose is to change the other person or their behavior, to vent or tell them off, then having this conversation is quite likely to produce many of the negative consequences you fear... if you're going to talk, talk. Really talk." (Ch. 7, Don't Just Vent / Don't Hit-and-Run)

plays.principle: Take Responsibility for Your Contribution Early
plays.what_it_means: When the seller genuinely shares fault (vague listing, slow ship, missed a flaw), own it up front and specifically — don't wait to be cornered into it.
plays.why_it_matters: Acknowledging your part early signals you've left the blame game, lowers the buyer's defensiveness, and prevents them from weaponizing it later. Competent people who own mistakes are seen as confident, not weak.
plays.when_to_use: Seller clearly contributed — late shipping, unlisted defect, misleading photo, wrong item sent.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When the seller did nothing wrong (don't over-apologize into implied liability); when fraud is alleged and admitting 'contribution' could be misread as admitting fraud — be precise about what you own.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Opening a complaint reply by owning the specific miss ('I should've photographed that scuff'), which de-escalates and often shrinks the buyer's demand.
plays.example_buyer_message: There's a stain on the inside you didn't mention!!
plays.recommended_agent_reply: You're right and that's on me — I missed that stain in the listing and should have flagged it. I'm sorry. I'd like to make it fair: a partial refund to keep it, or a full refund on return — your call.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "One of the best ways to signal your intention to leave behind the question of who's to blame is to acknowledge your own contribution early in the conversation." (Ch. 4, Take Responsibility for Your Contribution Early)

plays.principle: Speak for Yourself with "I feel / I'm seeing" — Not "You are"
plays.what_it_means: Express the seller's position as their own perception/feeling, not as truth or a character verdict about the buyer. 'I feel uneasy doing this off-platform' beats 'You're trying to scam me.' 'Here's what I'm seeing in the photos' beats 'You're lying.'
plays.why_it_matters: 'You are X' is a judgment that provokes 'Says who?!' and a fight. 'I feel / I see / my view is' can't be argued with and keeps the channel open. You're the authority on your experience, not on who's right.
plays.when_to_use: off-platform-request refusals, trust-authenticity, condition disputes — anywhere the seller must push back without accusing.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: When plain facts suffice ('tracking shows delivered') — don't soften a verifiable fact into a mere feeling.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Declining a 'let's do this on WhatsApp/PayPal F&F' request, or pushing back on a false damage claim, without calling the buyer a scammer.
plays.example_buyer_message: Just send it to my address and I'll Venmo you outside the app, trust me.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I appreciate you wanting to make it easy! I feel more comfortable keeping it on the app though — it protects us both if anything goes sideways. Happy to go ahead at the agreed price right here whenever you're ready.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "Beginning with 'I feel...' (not 'I feel like')... keeps the focus on feelings and makes clear that you are speaking only from your perspective. It avoids the translation trap of judging or accusing." (Ch. 5 / Ch. 10, Don't Present Your Conclusions as The Truth)

plays.principle: You Can't Change Them — Manage Yourself, Not Their Reaction
plays.what_it_means: Drop the goal of 'get the buyer to calm down / be reasonable / not leave a bad review.' You can only control your own clear, kind, firm reply. Measure success by whether you said it well, not by whether they reacted well.
plays.why_it_matters: Trying to control the buyer's reaction adds pressure, leads to over-conceding ('anything to avoid the hassle'), and rewards bad behavior. Releasing it is liberating and keeps the seller grounded enough to hold a fair line even if the buyer stays angry.
plays.when_to_use: Seller-coaching during hostile/abusive threads; when the user is panicking about a threatened bad review or dispute.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: n/a — mindset rail. It does not mean ignore the buyer; you still listen and offer fair fixes — you just don't contort yourself to manufacture their mood.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Coaching a seller through an abusive buyer threatening 1-star feedback — hold a fair, documented offer; accept you can't force the buyer to be happy.
plays.example_buyer_message: Refund me double or I'll leave you a 1-star and tell everyone you're a fraud.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: (copilot to seller) You can't control whether they leave a review or how they feel — only what you offer and how you say it. Stay fair and on the record: 'I understand you're upset. I can offer a full refund on return, which is fair. I'm not able to refund double. I've kept our messages and photos in case the platform needs to review it.' Then let the platform's process protect you.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "Just as you can't change another person, you can't control their reaction — and you shouldn't try... Don't measure the success of the conversation by whether or not they get upset." (Ch. 6, Let Go of Trying to Control Their Reaction)

plays.principle: Problem-Solve with Options + Fair Standards (not a willpower contest)
plays.what_it_means: Once feelings are handled, resolve the substance by inventing options that meet both sides' core concerns and anchoring on a fair standard (platform policy, return window, comparable resolutions) rather than haggling or out-stubborning the buyer.
plays.why_it_matters: It takes two to agree — you don't have to keep conceding until satisfied. Two options give the buyer agency; a fair standard lets either side back down without losing face. 'Mutual caretaking': relationships that always go one way don't last (= repeat buyers, good reviews).
plays.when_to_use: After acknowledgment, to land the resolution — refunds, returns, partials, replacements, price on Best-Offer.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Before feelings are acknowledged (premature problem-solving short-circuits resolution).
plays.mechanic: 1) Acknowledge feeling. 2) Name the shared goal ('let's get this fair for both of us'). 3) Offer 2 concrete options that protect your core interest. 4) If they reject both, cite a fair standard (platform return policy, listed measurements, agreed price). 5) If still no agreement, state your alternative calmly (let the platform's resolution process decide).
plays.marketplace_mapping: Closing a return/refund negotiation on Posh/eBay/Vinted by offering 'full refund on return OR keep-it partial,' citing the platform's buyer-protection terms as the neutral standard.
plays.example_buyer_message: I want a refund but I don't want to pay to ship it back.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: Totally fair to not want shipping hassle. Two options: I send you a prepaid return label and refund you in full once it's back, or you keep it and I refund you 25% now — that's in line with how the platform usually handles a minor not-as-described. Which sounds better?
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "problem-solving consists of gathering information... creating options that would meet both sides' primary concerns, and, where you can't find such options, seeking fair ways to resolve the difference... the principle of mutual caretaking." (Ch. 11, Now What? Begin to Problem-Solve)

plays.principle: Hit Pause Before Replying to a Triggering Message
plays.what_it_means: When an incoming message provokes a strong negative emotion, don't reply instantly. Wait, re-read when calmer, and question your attribution about what the sender 'meant.' Text strips tone — your worst-case reading is as likely wrong as right.
plays.why_it_matters: In text, intentions are invisible and we default to negative readings when stressed; an instant angry reply locks in an irreversible escalation. A pause usually reveals the message wasn't as hostile as it first felt — and protects against an on-the-record outburst.
plays.when_to_use: rude-aggressive inbound; ambiguous terse messages ('k.' / 'seriously?'); any reply the seller is tempted to send while angry.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: Genuinely time-sensitive logistics (address before same-day ship) — but 'I'm mad right now' never counts as urgent.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Copilot built-in cool-down: on an angry seller draft or hostile inbound, suggest waiting and offer a calmer drafted reply rather than sending in the heat of the moment.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: (copilot to seller) Hard to tell their tone from two words — could be anger, could be sarcasm, could be nothing about you. Let's not guess and over-react. Quick clarifier keeps it cool: 'Hey — I can't quite read this one. Is something wrong with the order? Happy to help sort it.'
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "If an email or text elicits a strong negative emotion, stop. Do nothing... Question Your Attributions... your initial reading is as likely to be off target as on." (FAQ 7, When Reading)

plays.principle: Name the Dynamic When Nothing Else Works
plays.what_it_means: When repeated listening/reframing fails and the buyer keeps attacking, bullying, or using ultimatums, put the pattern itself on the table as the topic: describe what's happening neutrally, decline to reward it, and (if needed) state consequences — without attributing intent.
plays.why_it_matters: It surfaces an unproductive pattern (e.g., 'agree with me or I leave a bad review') for honest discussion and neutralizes pressure tactics: you can't be forced to agree, and naming 'deciding by who threatens loudest' exposes how unworkable it is. Last-resort tool — it can raise tension, so use after good-faith attempts.
plays.when_to_use: Persistent bullying, repeated ultimatums/threats, buyer who keeps derailing; only after listening + reframing have failed.
plays.when_NOT_to_use: As a first move (too confrontational early); never pair it with an intent accusation ('you're trying to bully me') — describe the behavior, not their motive.
plays.marketplace_mapping: Handling a buyer who repeatedly threatens negative feedback to extract extra money — name the pattern, hold a fair offer, document, before involving platform support.
plays.example_buyer_message: I'll keep leaving bad reviews on all your listings until you pay me $50 extra.
plays.recommended_agent_reply: I want to help with the actual problem with your order, and I notice we keep landing on 'pay more or get bad reviews.' I'm not able to make decisions that way — it wouldn't be fair to either of us. Here's my genuine offer to fix the issue: [option]. If the reviews continue, I'll report the thread to the platform, since this crosses into feedback extortion. I'd much rather just resolve it.
plays.source_quote_or_locator: "naming the dynamic can help. You put on the table as a topic for discussion what you see happening in the conversation itself... avoid the attribution of intent." (Ch. 11 + FAQ 2, Name the Dynamic / Clarify Consequences)

communication_tactics.name: Acknowledgment opener
communication_tactics.purpose: Validate the buyer's feeling before anything else
phrase_patterns: It sounds like this matters a lot to you.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Sounds canned/patronizing if used on calm buyers or stacked; feelings must be genuine.
communication_tactics.good_example: That's frustrating after waiting two weeks — I'd be annoyed too. Let's fix it.
communication_tactics.bad_example: Calm down, it's not a big deal.

communication_tactics.name: "I feel / I'm seeing" framing
communication_tactics.purpose: State the seller's view as perception, not a verdict on the buyer
phrase_patterns: Here's what I'm seeing in the photos ___.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Don't water down verifiable facts into mere 'feelings.'
communication_tactics.good_example: I feel safer keeping this on the app.
communication_tactics.bad_example: You're obviously trying to scam me.
communication_tactics.source_locator: Ch. 10, Don't Present Conclusions as Truth

communication_tactics.name: The And-Stance connector
communication_tactics.purpose: Hold a firm line while validating the buyer
phrase_patterns: I understand ___, AND I'm not able to ___, AND I do want to help.
communication_tactics.good_example: I get $15 is your budget, and I can do $25, and no pressure.
communication_tactics.bad_example: Either you pay full or forget it.

communication_tactics.name: Impact-not-intent separator
communication_tactics.purpose: Respond to harm without conceding bad intent
phrase_patterns: I hear you feel ___; that wasn't my intention, and I want to make it right.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: If used as a rote formula it sounds evasive; acknowledge the harm concretely.
communication_tactics.good_example: I hear you feel cheated — I didn't sell it believing it was fake, and I'll get to the bottom of it.
communication_tactics.bad_example: I would NEVER scam anyone, how dare you.
communication_tactics.source_locator: Ch. 3, Speak to the Impact; Inquire About Intentions

communication_tactics.name: Open-ended diagnostic question
communication_tactics.purpose: Surface the real issue before reacting
phrase_patterns: Can you tell me more about what's off?
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Endless questions used to avoid delivering news ('easing in').
communication_tactics.good_example: Help me understand what's wrong and I'll fix it.
communication_tactics.source_locator: Ch. 9, Inquire to Learn / Ask Open-Ended Questions

communication_tactics.name: Paraphrase-back
communication_tactics.purpose: Prove you heard, stop the buyer from repeating
phrase_patterns: Let me make sure I've got it: ___.
So part of the problem is ___?
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Sounds mechanical if it's a word-for-word echo; restate in your own words.
communication_tactics.good_example: Let me check I've got it: arrived damaged, you want it sorted, not a policy lecture.
communication_tactics.bad_example: Like I already said, my policy is...

communication_tactics.name: Two-option close
communication_tactics.purpose: Give the buyer agency while protecting the seller's interest
phrase_patterns: Two options: ___ or ___. Which works better for you?
communication_tactics.good_example: Full refund on return, or keep it and I refund 25% now — your call.

communication_tactics.name: Fair-standard anchor
phrase_patterns: That's in line with how the platform usually handles ___.
communication_tactics.overuse_risk: Citing rules before feelings (cold); cherry-picking a weak standard.
communication_tactics.good_example: A partial here is in line with the platform's not-as-described norm.
communication_tactics.source_locator: Ch. 11, Ask What Standards Should Apply

objection_material.handling: Hit Pause + question your attribution (play-14); acknowledge the feeling, don't reply in kind; if it persists, Name the Dynamic + clarify consequences (play-15). Don't reward bad behavior, don't play their game.
objection_material.sample_reply: I want to help with your order. Let's keep it on the actual problem — here's what I can do: [option].
objection_material.source_locator: FAQ 2, What Might Help; Ch. 11, Name the Dynamic

objection_material.handling: Disentangle intent from impact (play-02): acknowledge they feel cheated, don't protest innocence first; invite evidence; speak in 'I'm seeing' not 'you're lying' (play-11).
objection_material.sample_reply: I hear you think it's not authentic and feel cheated — I take that seriously. Can you send photos of the date code? If it's not right, I'll make it right.

objection_material.handling: Move to curiosity (play-07); frame as different stories / joint contribution (play-03), not 'you're wrong'; offer fair options (play-13).
objection_material.sample_reply: I don't want you unhappy with the condition. Can you show me what's concerning you? We may have read the photos differently — let's sort it fairly.
objection_material.source_locator: Ch. 2, Move from Certainty to Curiosity; Ch. 4, Contribution

objection_material.handling: And Stance (play-05): validate budget, hold price warmly, offer one small move. Express price as your view + reason, not a verdict on them.
objection_material.sample_reply: I get that's your budget, and I've priced it at $28 for [reason] — I can meet at $25. No pressure either way.

objection_material.handling: Share where your conclusion comes from (the listing rationale) as your view, not truth; And Stance to hold firm without souring.
objection_material.sample_reply: I hear it feels high. My view is it's fair given [condition/brand/comps], and I'd rather be upfront than haggle. Let me know if it works for you.
objection_material.source_locator: Ch. 10, Share Where Your Conclusions Come From

objection_material.handling: Problem-solve with options + fair standard; mutual caretaking (offer a reasonable joint win).
objection_material.sample_reply: Happy to bundle — if you take both, I can do $X total, which is a fair break for buying two. Want me to set that up?

objection_material.handling: Open from the Third Story (play-04) with a no-blame nudge; don't hit-and-run (play-09); make it safe to say no.
objection_material.sample_reply: Wires might've crossed on timing — I've got it held for you. Totally fine either way, just let me know if you'd still like it.
objection_material.source_locator: Ch. 8, Begin from the Third Story

objection_material.handling: Speak for yourself with 'I feel' (play-11); decline warmly without accusing; offer to proceed on-platform.
objection_material.sample_reply: Appreciate you wanting to make it easy! I feel more comfortable keeping it on the app — protects us both. Happy to go ahead right here.
objection_material.source_locator: Ch. 5 / Ch. 10, Say "I feel" not "You are"

objection_material.handling: Acknowledge feeling first (play-01); own your contribution if you were slow (play-10); offer options.
objection_material.sample_reply: Sorry it's taken this long — that's on me. I can refund part of the shipping, or expedite the next step. Which helps more?

objection_material.handling: And Stance + fair-standard framing; don't disparage; hold value as your view.
objection_material.sample_reply: Totally fair to compare. Mine's priced for [condition/authenticity/included extras] — I think it's fair, and I'm happy to do $X if that closes the gap for you.

objection_material.handling: Low emotional content — not this book's strength. Answer plainly and warmly; no acknowledgment theater needed.
objection_material.sample_reply: Yes, still available! Want me to send an offer or hold it for you?
objection_material.source_locator: Ch. 5, Sometimes Feelings Are Best Left Out

buyer_psychology: Buyers attribute intent from impact: feeling cheated -> 'you cheated me.' The accusation reflects their hurt, not real knowledge of your motives.
When stressed/tired, buyers read negative intent into ambiguous text (terse messages, no emoji, all-caps) more readily — the medium amplifies suspicion.
Buyers who keep repeating themselves are signaling they don't feel heard, not that they're stupid; paraphrasing them ends the loop.
An 'obvious self-serving lie' is surprisingly often the buyer's actual belief — they may genuinely think the item is fake/damaged.
Buyers have invisible questions behind every complaint: 'Do you care? Can I trust you?' A defensive first reply silently answers 'no.'
Buyers behaving badly usually feel their behavior is justified (by how they think you treated them or fear of being taken advantage of) — there's a logic you can engage.
People rarely change because you tell them to; they open up when they feel understood first ('conversational receptiveness' makes you more persuasive).
A buyer's anger can spring from fear (of being scammed, of losing money) — addressing the fear defuses aggression faster than defending yourself.
seller_psychology: Truth assumption — seller assumes 'I'm right, buyer's wrong.' Disputes are rarely about facts; they're about interpretation and what feels fair. Counter with curiosity.
Intention invention — seller assumes the buyer is 'out to scam me' and treats that guess as fact; assumes the worst when frustrated.
Good-intentions defense — seller rushes to 'I'd never scam you / I described it accurately,' dismissing the buyer's experience and escalating. Acknowledge first.
Blame frame — seller wants the buyer to 'admit it's their fault'; triggers defensiveness and stalls resolution. Shift to contribution.
Venting / hit-and-run urge — seller wants a satisfying jab or snippy one-liner; predictably backfires into bad reviews and disputes.
Trying to control the buyer's reaction — leads to over-conceding 'to avoid the hassle,' which rewards bad behavior and trains repeat extortion.
All-or-nothing identity — a complaint feels like 'I'm a bad/incompetent seller,' knocking the seller into denial or panic-refunding. Ground the identity: one complaint isn't who you are.
Treating self charitably, others harshly — seller forgives their own slow shipping ('I was busy') but reads the buyer's lateness as disrespect.
Reacting instantly to triggering messages — sends an irreversible angry reply before the cool-down that would have softened the read.
ignore_list: Deep interpersonal/relational contexts (marriages, parenting, firing, grief, estate disputes, trauma, abuse, mental-illness caregiving, workplace racial-justice conversations) — rich examples, out of scope beyond transferable mechanics.
In-person / video / phone channel guidance — P2P resale is async chat; kept only the text-specific rules (pause, question attributions, be explicit, don't leave them hanging).
Identity-grounding as personal growth (lifelong self-acceptance, asking for help, forgiveness, letting go) — kept only the operational slice: 'one complaint isn't your whole identity.'
Truth/relativism and political-polarization FAQ — philosophical, not actionable for resale.
Organizational-culture / 'bring feelings to work' material — enterprise context, dropped.
Empathy-as-lifelong-journey philosophy — inspiring but not a product behavior.
guardrail_flags: Anti-manipulation by design — almost nothing here is ethically dicey; risk is the opposite of Influence/Voss.

Don't fake feelings: acknowledgment and 'I feel' statements only work if genuine. A copilot generating hollow 'I totally understand how you feel' boilerplate is manipulative and reads fake — tie acknowledgment to a specific stated grievance or skip it.

Don't weaponize 'contribution'/'And Stance' to dodge real fault. If the seller actually shipped a broken/fake item, 'we both contributed' or 'and I'm still firm' is gaslighting — use clean ownership (play-10). Never apply 'contribution' to a genuinely defrauded buyer.
Consequences (play-15) are fine ('I'll report this to the platform'); threats, retaliation, or matching abuse are not — keep the copilot from 'playing their game.'
Off-platform refusal (play-11) doubles as a fraud-safety rail — keep it firm; never let warmth talk the seller into off-platform payment.

classifiers_personas.name: Accuser (intent-attribution)
signals: loaded verbs implying deliberateness ('you excluded/scammed/tricked me')
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Disentangle intent from impact (play-02): acknowledge the harm/feeling, set the intent-accusation aside, invite evidence.

classifiers_personas.name: Blamer (either-or fault)
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Reframe blame -> contribution (play-03/06); own your real part early (play-10); pivot to a forward fix.

classifiers_personas.name: Bully / ultimatum-giver
signals: rapid escalation when pushed back on
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Don't reward / don't react in kind; if persistent, Name the Dynamic + clarify consequences + document (play-15).

classifiers_personas.name: Fear-driven (suspicious)
signals: 'how do I know this is real?'
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Recognize aggression-from-fear; validate and address the fear, invite verification, stay calm (play-07, play-02).
classifiers_personas.source_locator: FAQ 3, Recognize That Aggression Can Spring from Fear

classifiers_personas.name: Unheard / looping
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Listen + paraphrase before re-explaining (play-08); they engage once they feel heard.

classifiers_personas.name: Ambiguous-terse
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Hit Pause + question attribution (play-14); send a short, neutral clarifier before assuming hostility.

classifiers_personas.name: Calm / transactional
signals: logistics only ('is this available?', 'ship to X?')
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Skip the emotional layer entirely; answer plainly and warmly — acknowledgment theater here is counterproductive.
classifiers_personas.source_locator: Ch. 5, Sometimes Feelings Are Best Left Out

classifiers_personas.name: Seller-side: Shifter vs Absorber (coaching persona)
signals: Shifter: blames the buyer/circumstances, misses own contribution, feels like a victim
Absorber: over-apologizes, panic-refunds, lets bad behavior slide, feels responsible for everything
classifiers_personas.recommended_approach: Nudge each toward the balanced middle: prompt the shifter to spot their own contribution; help the absorber hold a fair line and not reward bad behavior.
classifiers_personas.source_locator: Ch. 4, Map the Contribution System (Shifters and Absorbers)
